claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

**First, let me ask about the child (or children) you're inquiring about:**

How old is the child? The age determines the basic eligibility path:
- **Under 16**: Generally qualifies automatically
- **16-19 years old**: May qualify if in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Not eligible

Could you tell me:
1. How many children are you asking about?
2. What is the age of each child?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm asking about my child Alex, who is 14 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me assess the eligibility for your child Alex, who is 14 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification age category. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's work status:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

In other words, is Alex either:
- Not working at all, OR
- Working less than 24 hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at all. They're just focused on school at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex isn't working at all, that's fine. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Tax Credits

in their own name (not as part of your household claim). 

Most 14-year-olds don't receive these benefits. Does Alex receive any benefits in their own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about apprenticeships:

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

If yes, I'll need to know which type, as some apprenticeships disqualify from Child Benefit:
- **All apprenticeships in England** disqualify (except Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales are approved)
- **Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland** disqualify
- **Most apprenticeships in Wales** disqualify (except Foundation level)

Since Alex is 14 and focused on school, I'm assuming they're not in an apprenticeship. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship. They're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex is not in an apprenticeship, let's move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status:

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK, or does any of these other situations apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Since you're living abroad, I need to check if you fall into any of the special circumstances that would still allow you to claim Child Benefit.

**Are you living abroad in any of these situations?**

1. **Crown servant** - Working for the UK government abroad (e.g., diplomatic service, armed forces)
2. **Living in certain qualifying countries** - Some specific arrangements may apply depending on where you are

Could you tell me:
- Where are you currently living?
- What is your employment or reason for living abroad?
- Are you a Crown servant or working for the UK government abroad?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm living abroad for personal reasons, not for work with the UK government. I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Based on what you've told me, unfortunately you don't meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit.

Let me navigate to the outcome to provide you with the full information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Eligibility Decision: NOT ELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for Alex.

### **Why you're not eligible:**

While Alex meets all the requirements as a qualifying child (being 14 years old, in school, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship), **you don't meet the UK residency requirements**.

To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- Normally live in the UK, OR
- Have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with certain conditions), OR
- Be a Crown servant working abroad, OR
- Be living in certain qualifying countries under specific arrangements

Since you're living abroad for personal reasons and don't fall into any of these special categories, you cannot claim Child Benefit at this time.

### **What you could do:**

- **If you return to live in the UK**, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex at that point
- If your circumstances change (e.g., you become a Crown servant), you may become eligible
- You may want to check if you're entitled to any family benefits in the country where you're currently residing

### **More information:**
You can find more details about Child Benefit eligibility at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility

I understand this isn't the answer you were hoping for. If you have any questions or if your circumstances change, please don't hesitate to ask.

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - all of which would normally qualify. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant or working for the UK government abroad, nor do they have settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. Therefore, despite Alex being a qualifying child, the claimant is ineligible for Child Benefit due to not meeting the residency requirements.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child (Alex, age 14) who meets all the child-related eligibility criteria. However, the claim fails at the claimant residency requirement stage. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and does not meet any of the special circumstances that would allow claiming while residing outside the UK.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant or working for the UK government abroad, nor do they have settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. Therefore, despite Alex being a qualifying child, the claimant is ineligible for Child Benefit due to not meeting the residency requirements.
